2005-05-01.title: U.S. Recruits a Rough Ally to Be a Jailer
2005-05-01.abstract: Seven months before Sept. 11, 2001, the State Department issued a human rights report on Uzbekistan. It was a litany of horrors.    The police repeatedly tortured prisoners, State Department officials wrote, noting that the most common techniques were ''beating, often with blunt weapons, and asphyxiation with a gas mask.'' Separately, international human rights groups had reported that torture in Uzbek jails included boiling of body parts, using electroshock on genitals and plucking off fingernails and toenails with pliers. Two prisoners were boiled to death, the groups reported. The February 2001 State Department report stated bluntly, ''Uzbekistan is an authoritarian state with limited civil rights.''

2005-05-01.title: Insurgents unleash third day of deadly attacks in defiance of Iraq's new government
Attacks Batter Iraqi Civilians
2005-05-01.abstract: Bombing attacks killed more than 15 Iraqis on Saturday in the capital and in the northern part of the country, and three civilians in Falluja were killed in rocket attacks by guerrillas, officials said.     The attacks included a suicide car bomb aimed at a Sunni political figure on his way to a meeting in Baghdad, the Interior Ministry said. The leader was not killed.

2005-05-01.title: Violence in Iraq Claims More Than 100 Lives in Past 3 Days
A Jolt to Team Japan: Bonus Demands
2005-05-01.abstract: In a country better known for copying and improving others' creations, the invention that was born here in rural Japan in 1993, at a 400-employee company surrounded by farmland, promised nothing less than a better light bulb.    In fact, it was the Holy Grail of lighting technology: the blue light-emitting diode, or L.E.D., which made white light a reality. It offers the prospect of a cheap, long-lasting light source that is expected to, someday soon, replace Edison's incandescent light bulb.

2005-05-02.title: Berlusconi May Be Down, but It's Too Soon to Count Him Out
2005-05-02.abstract: Prime Min Silvio Berlusconi's enemies and allies agree that his air of invincibility has deflated after center-right's heavy losses in regional elections last month; but no one is counting him out; for years he has confounded his opponents, in Italy and around Europe, with combination of poliitical smarts, brio and luck that seemed inexhaustible; photo (M)

2005-05-02.title: Broke but dynamic, Berlin seeks new identity
Sharansky Is Quitting Israel's Cabinet
2005-05-02.abstract: Natan Sharansky, Soviet dissident turned Israeli politician whose ideas on promotion of democracy have been endorsed by Pres Bushm, says he will resign from Prime Min Ariel Sharon's cabinet because he opposes Israel's unilateral withdrawal from Gaza; cites lack of linkage of Gaza withdrawal to specific reforms to create more freedom in Palestinian society; says he cannot remain in government devoted almost solely now to carrying out Gaza withdrawal; he wants international aid linked to promotion of free Palestinian press and economy, new school textbooks that do not promote anti-Semitic or anti-Israel themes, dismantling of terrorist organizations and replacement of squalid refugee camps with decent housing; says only when Palestinians'lives are improved will there be chance for true peace; photo (M)

2005-05-02.title: Key senator signals he's unsure on Bolton
Egypt Detains More Than 200 in Attacks on Tourist Industry
2005-05-02.abstract: Egyptian authorities sweep through villages on outskirts of Cairo, detaining more than 200 people day after attacks that left seven people wounded and three attackers dead; police say most detainees are being investigated for connection to local terrorist networks; say one of dead attackers was suspect in deadly bombing Apr 7 at Cairo's main bazaar (M)

2005-05-03.title: Pentagon Says Iraq Effort Limits Ability to Fight Other Conflicts
2005-05-03.abstract: Chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff Gen Richard B Myers reportedly tells Congress in classified report that Pentagon's ability to deal with potential armed conflicts around world would be limited because of concentration of American troops and weapons in Iraq and Afghanistan; says any major combat operations elsewhere in world would probably be more protracted and produce higher American and foreign civilian casualties as result; says factors that would limit Pentagon's ability to prevail as quickly as war planners once predicted in other potential conflicts include reduced stockpiles of precision weapons, which were depleted during invasion of Iraq, and stress on reserve units, which fulfill bulk of combat support duties in Iraq; but despite limitations, Myers is unwavering in his assessment that American forces would win any major combat operation and are 'fully capable' of meeting all Washington's military objectives; just last week Pres Bush claimed that number of American troops in Iraq would not limit Washington's military options elsewhere; photo (M)

2005-05-03.title: Philippines judged 'most murderous' place for journalists
World Briefing | Asia: Afghanistan: 85 Afghans Freed
2005-05-03.abstract: The United States military released 85 Afghan prisoners from detention at its two main air bases in Bagram and Kandahar on Sunday and handed them over to Afghan authorities. They were mostly from south and southeastern Afghanistan and had been held for up to two years. They met with the country's chief justice, who urged them to participate in the peace process and reconstruction before he allowed them to go home.   Carlotta Gall (NYT)

2005-05-03.title: EU hopes to soften Japan fears over China

2005-05-04.title: Victory Day holiday inflames old wounds
House and Senate Reach Accord on $82 Billion for Costs of Wars
2005-05-04.abstract: House and Senate conference agrees to final version of $82 billion supplementary spending bill for wars in Iraq and Afghanistan; measure includes $75.9 billion for military operations as well as other money to increase death benefits to military families, to bolster United States border security, and to provide aid to Palestinians (S)

2005-05-04.title: Army rigged Guantánamo interrogations, author says

2005-05-05.title: Iraq war could steal votes for Blair
World Briefing | Americas: Canada: No Appeal Of Verdict In Air India Bombing
2005-05-05.abstract: Prosecutors in Vancouver said they will not appeal the acquittal of two Sikh men who were charged in the 1985 bombing of Air India Flight 182, which exploded and crashed off Ireland while flying from Toronto to India, killing 329. In a ruling in March, the judge in the case said the prosecution's chief witnesses against the two men, Ripudaman Singh Malik, 58, and Ajaib Singh Bagri, 55, were not credible. Prosecutors have now concluded that there were no legal errors in the ruling that would allow an appeal. The decision was met with disappointment from the victim's families, who continue to demand a federal inquiry into the failure of the 20-year case.   Colin Campbell (NYT)

2005-05-05.title: A Setback to Democrats' Request for Papers on Bolton and Syria
2005-05-05.abstract: Republican chairman of Senate Foreign Relations Committee declines to endorse Democratic request that State Dept turn over documents related to long-running dispute between John R Bolton and American intelligence agencies over assessments about Syria and its weapons program; move is blow to Democrats on panel, who have focused on dispute as central part of their effort to defeat Bolton's nomination as ambassador to United Nations (M)

2005-05-05.title: Heavy fighting in the southeastern Afghanistan
As Britain Votes, Tight Races for Older Voters Hold the Key
2005-05-05.abstract: Labor Party is expected to be overall winner in British elections, but lackluster win would have far-reaching political consequences for Prime Min Tony Blair; key to outcome is likely to be in places where Labor Party has only slender lead on Conservatives and where older people are far more likely to determine outcome of vote than young; photo (M)

2005-05-06.title: Insurgents Press Attacks on Iraq Security Forces, Killing 22
2005-05-06.abstract: At least 22 people are killed when Iraqi insurgents stage series of attacks across Baghdad, including suicide attack amid crowd of job applicants outside Iraqi Army base; insurgents have seized on indecision of new Shiite-led government and Sunni Arabs over unfilled cabinet posts to mount increasinlgy deadly attacks; in Mosul, car bomb kills four policemen; photo (M)

2005-05-06.title: Acquittal denied, defense in Jackson case takes stage
Malays reject accusations they aid Thai separatists
News Analysis: With Qaeda's No. 3 in jail, U.S. could get vital details

2005-05-07.title: A Wounded Blair, Rivals Strengthened, Begins Third Term; Tory Leader to Step Down
2005-05-07.abstract: Britain's political landscape was redrawn on Friday after a tumultuous election that gave Prime Minister Tony Blair a third term with a severely reduced majority, strengthening rivals within and outside his Labor Party, and hastened the departure of Michael Howard as the leader of the opposition Conservatives.    Mr. Howard announced that he would step down ''sooner rather than later'' after he increased his party's representation in the 646-seat Parliament but failed to ignite broader appeal beyond the bedrock Conservative base.

2005-05-07.title: Leonid Shamkovich, 81, Ex-Soviet Chess Grandmaster, Is Dead
2005-05-07.abstract: Leonid Shamkovich, Russian-born chess grandmaster, dies at age 81 (M)

2005-05-07.title: Briefly: EU reports evidence of fraud in Togo vote
Names of the Dead
2005-05-07.abstract: The Department of Defense has identified 1,589 American service members who have died since the start of the Iraq war. It confirmed the death of the following American yesterday:      BROOKS, William J., 30, Staff Sgt., Army; Birmingham, Ala.; Third Armored Division.

2005-05-08.title: Tug of War: Intelligence vs. Politics
2005-05-08.abstract: For more than two years, critics who accused the Bush administration of improperly using political influence to shape intelligence assessments have, for the most part, failed to make the charge stick. On Iraq, the main focus of scrutiny, two official inquiries have blamed intelligence agencies for inflating the threat posed by Baghdad's illicit weapons, but have stopped short of blaming political pressures for the problem.    Those findings have never fully satisfied many intelligence officials and some administration critics. At minimum, they have said, some senior Bush administration officials have played an unhelpful role, by urging intelligence agencies to revise conclusions in a direction more consistent with administration policy, as in pursuing links between Iraq and Al Qaeda.

2005-05-08.title: Amid Tensions, Bush and Putin Conduct Meeting in Russia
2005-05-08.abstract: The leaders met in what was widely expected to be a tense encounter, but advisers to both men moved swiftly to pronounce the meeting a success.

2005-05-08.title: Eleven killed, 162 wounded in rebel bombings in Myanmar capital
Chair of Senate Intelligence Committee Believes North Korea Is Preparing Nuclear Test
2005-05-08.abstract: WASHINGTON, May 8 - The chairman of the Senate intelligence committee said today that he believed North Korea appeared to be preparing a nuclear test explosion, even as the head of the United Nations nuclear monitoring agency said he believed the North had five or six nuclear weapons in its possession.

2005-05-08.title: Hard-Liners in Northern Ireland Gain in British Voting, Leaving Peace Pact in Jeopardy
2005-05-08.abstract: Voters in Northern Ireland have delivered convincing victories to hard-line parties in Britain's parliamentary elections, stoking fears that a resumption of the suspended Northern Irish legislature, which is intended to provide power-sharing between Roman Catholic and Protestant groups, will be more difficult.     The results moved Northern Ireland closer to a two-party system after decades in which voters could choose from a spectrum of moderate opinion.

2005-05-09.title: Blair Brushes Aside Stereotypes to Praise British-German Relations
2005-05-09.abstract: The British prime minister gave his first foreign interview to the German newspaper Bild Zeitung, a racy daily known for reducing foreign policy issues to basics.

2005-05-09.title: Guest List at Putin's Party Could Become an Event
2005-05-09.abstract: Bush administration officials say they have no idea where Pres Bush's place will be, who he will stand near, at Pres Vladimir V Putin's celebration of 60th anniversary of German defeat in World War II; there is speculation about what he would say if he stands near any foreign leader he has called a dictator (White House Letter) (M)

2005-05-09.title: Issue in China: Many in Jails Without Trial
2005-05-09.abstract: China is being pressured to abolish or change its vast system of re-education camps that operate outside court system; estimated 300,000 prostitutes, drug users, petty criminals, members of Falun Gong and other political prisoners have been stripped of any legal rights and locked inside vast penal system that is separate from judicial system; system, relic of Mao era, is major tool Communist Party uses to maintain hold on power and social order; it allows police to sweep up masses of people without time and complications of court trials; China is facing pressure at home and abroad to change; key members of European Union express reluctance to lift arms embargo without significant gesture by China on human rights; outside China, Falun Gong is waging aggressive campaign to pubilcize its allegations of mistreatment, which Chinese government denies; history of labor re-education camps detailed; photo; map (M)

2005-05-09.title: Rice's Reason for Withholding Bolton Files: A Chilling Effect
2005-05-09.abstract: State Dept refuses to make public internal documents sought by Senate Democrats in their attempt to seek more information about repeated clashes between John R Bolton and American intelligence agencies over Syria; Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice says information involves 'internal deliberations' and their disclosure could have chilling effect on debates within administration; Bolton has been chosen by Pres Bush to be ambassador to United Nations (M)

2005-05-10.title: 100 Rebels Killed in U.S. Offensive in Western Iraq
2005-05-10.abstract: Marine task force sweeps through wide area of westen Iraq near Syrian border, killing 100 insurgents and raiding desert outposts and city safe houses they use to import cars, money, weapons and foreigners to fight United States and Iraqi forces; senior American commanders blame porous border with Syria for allowing never-ending stream of armed jihadists to enter Iraq and replenish insurgency as quickly as fighters are killed or captured; say new offensive, largest since Marines invaded Falluja six months ago, was spurred by new intelligence gleaned from Iraqis living in area as well as interrogations of newly captured aides to Abu Musab al-Zarqawi, most wanted terrorist in Iraq; Iraq's new defense minister Sadoon al-Dulaimi says he will continue military policies set by departing administration of Ayad Allawi, who aggressively pursued operations against insurgents; photo; map (M)

2005-05-10.title: U.S. Says Fighting Pauses in Western Iraq as Rebels Regroup
2005-05-10.abstract: Insurgents who control parts of the western Iraqi desert appeared to have fallen back as the Marines prepared a new assault.

2005-05-10.title: World Briefing | Americas: Mexico: Mayor To Quit To Seek Presidency
2005-05-10.abstract: Mayor Andrés Manuel López Obrador of Mexico City said he would step down on July 31 to begin campaigning for his party's presidential nomination. Mr. López Obrador, of the leftist Democratic Revolutionary Party, leads all national polls ahead of next year's election. His popularity soared even as the federal government sought to prosecute him over a minor land dispute. It dropped the charges last week.   Antonio Betancourt (NYT)

2005-05-10.title: U.S. needs a 'break' in troop withdrawals
World Briefing | Europe: Spain: Measures To Combat Drought
2005-05-10.abstract: After six months of exceptionally dry weather, the government has begun restricting water use in preparation for the first major drought in 10 years, an official at the Environmental Ministry said. Average precipitation across Spain this winter measured less than 3 inches, or 35 percent of typical levels. Forecasts show few signs that rainfall will increase significantly over the summer. The restrictions apply so far just to water used for irrigating crops. Officials in the agricultural industry, particularly in the olive and cereal sectors, have warned that their crops could be seriously damaged by the restrictions.   Renwick McLean (NYT)

2005-05-11.title: World Briefing | Asia: Nepal: U.S. Backs Fight Against Rebels
2005-05-11.abstract: Less than two weeks after suspending emergency rule, King Gyanendra got a nod of support from two of his most important allies. India announced that it would resume military aid, suspended since the king dismissed the government on Feb. 1 and took direct power, and the United States said it backed him against the country's Maoist insurgency. The American support came on a visit by the assistant secretary of state for South Asia, Christina Rocca, above. She is the highest ranking American official to visit since the royal takeover, and she urged the palace to free political prisoners, reinstate civil liberties and reach out to political parties. The United States also cut military aid, and she did not say when it might be restored.   Somini Sengupta (NYT)

2005-05-11.title: North Korea Says It Has Pulled Nuclear Rods From Reactor
2005-05-11.abstract: Prime Minister Junichiro Koizumi of Japan downplayed Pyongyang's statement, calling it "gamesmanship."

2005-05-11.title: Holocaust Memorial Opens in Berlin
2005-05-11.abstract: Germany's much debated and long-awaited Holocaust memorial opens, Berlin; Parliament president Wolfgang Thierse speaks, rite attended Chanceller Gerhard Schroder and all senior government officials; Peter Eisenman designed memorial; photo (M)

2005-05-11.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Arson Suspected In Synagogue Fire
2005-05-11.abstract: A fire that gutted a historic synagogue in Malakhovka, just outside Moscow, may have been arson, a police official told the Itar-Tass news agency. A village official and Russia's chief rabbi, Berl Lazar, said they also believed it to be arson. The synagogue, which had also been burglarized over the weekend, was built to look like a shed in the 1930's, when religion was brutally persecuted in the Soviet Union.   Sophia Kishkovsky (NYT)

2005-05-11.title: Insurgents kill 60 in spate of attacks across country

2005-05-12.title: World Briefing | Europe: Ireland: Approval For Highway Near Ancient Site
2005-05-12.abstract: The environment minister, Dick Roche, issued licenses for the excavation of archaeological sites around the Hill of Tara, effectively approving construction of a highway that will run adjacent to Tara, the 4,000-year-old site that is the burial place of 140 kings. Hundreds of historians and archaeologists, and the government's cultural advisers, have campaigned against the roadway's path, and its planners would have been forced to explore an alternative route if Mr. Roche had withheld the licenses.   Brian Lavery (NYT)

2005-05-12.title: Putin Helps Bush Get Into Gear for Friendly Photo Opportunity
2005-05-12.abstract: Reporter's Notebook on Pres Bush's encounter with stick shift on Russian Pres Vladimir Putin's gleamingly restored 1956 Volga, parked outside Putin's dacha; American reporters with Bush in Moscow are barred from military parade marking 60th anniversary of defeat of Nazis, but amuse themselves by watching glitzy production on Russian television; cameras catch Bush with other leaders, although not any out-of-favor dictators or former dictators; Bush, traveling on to Georgia, commiserates with Pres Mikhail Saakashvili about press (M)

2005-05-12.title: Names of the Dead
2005-05-12.abstract: Names of four Americans newly confirmed dead in Iraq; 1,601 American service members have died since start of war

2005-05-12.title: Afghan Protest Against the U.S. Becomes Violent
2005-05-12.abstract: Four protesters are killed and more than 60 injured in Jalalabad as Afghan police and troops struggle to contain anti-American demonstrations; photo; map; religious hard-liners and armed man apparently usurp what started as student protest (M)

2005-05-13.title: German lower house approves EU charter
Court-Martial Opens in an Iraqi Abuse Case
2005-05-13.abstract: Military proseuctors in trial of Specialist Sabrina Harman, former Abu Ghraib prison guard, say photographs, including one of hooded detainee standing on box with wires in his hand, are irrefutable evidence of guard's guilt; case against Harman is largely based on photos she took or posed in; her defense team notes that Harman was lowest-ranking soldier among those prosecuted in scandal, and that she was repulsed by abuses which she reported to superiors, to no avail; photo (M)

2005-05-13.title: European Court Urges Turkey to Grant Kurdish Leader a New Trial
2005-05-13.abstract: European Human Rights Court upholds earlier decision that 1999 trial of Kurdish separatist leader Abdullah Ocalan was unfair and that Turkey should reopen case; cites extended period of time he was held before standing trial and limited time given for him to prepare defense; Turkey could jeopardize its application to join European Union if it refuses to revisit case; many people in Turkey worry that reopening case could reignite fires of Kurdish nationalism; it could also rekindle sense of Turkish nationalism, which could further complicate country's efforts to join EU; although court is not European Union body, defying its wishes could put Turkey in breach of European Convention on Human Rights, to which all EU members must belong (M)

2005-05-13.title: World Briefing | Europe: Italy: Conjugal Duty Is A Legal Duty, Court Rules
2005-05-13.abstract: Italy's highest court ruled that a man who married without telling his bride that he was impotent denied her the ''right to sexuality'' and possibly motherhood and ordered him to pay her damages, Italian news media reported. The amount of damages will be decided by another court. The couple married in the 1990's without having had sex, and the woman learned of her new husband's condition on their wedding night. The marriage was later annulled, but the woman demanded damages for his lack of ''fairness and good faith'' before their wedding and for his refusal to seek treatment afterward because his condition would then become known to someone else.   Jason Horowitz (NYT)

2005-05-14.title: Measured hopes for a fledging East Timor
'Day of Solidarity' Shouldn't Include Work, French Say
2005-05-14.abstract: In the name of fraternity, the French are feuding.    The bickering is over Pentecost, the Christian feast commemorating the descent of the Holy Spirit upon the apostles on the 50th day after the Resurrection of Christ. The day after, Pentecost Monday, has long been celebrated in France as a paid holiday.

2005-05-14.title: Tragicomedy of Life in Baghdad Is Brought Home in a TV Series
2005-05-14.abstract: One morning last month, Qasim al-Malakh, one of Iraq's best-known actors, stood near a dusty vacant lot in a dangerous part of southern Baghdad. He was dressed immaculately in a dark suit and tie, and his co-star, Nagham al-Sultani, stood next to him in a white bridal gown.    They were there to film the last episode of ''Love and War,'' Iraq's most popular television drama. Their characters, Fawzi and Fatin, had just married after a long and troubled romance.

2005-05-15.title: After Its Epidemic Arrival, SARS Vanishes
2005-05-15.abstract: Two and a half years after a mysterious respiratory illness from southern China infected thousands of people around the world and brought dire predictions of recurring and deadly plague, the virus known as SARS has again provided a surprise.    It has disappeared, at least for the moment.

2005-05-15.title: Names of the Dead
2005-05-15.abstract: The Department of Defense has identified 1,612 American service members who have died since the start of the Iraq war. It confirmed the deaths of the following Americans on Friday:    JODON, Andrew R., 27, Sgt., Army; Karthaus, Pa.; Third Infantry Division.    ZEIGLER, Kenneth E. II, 22, Pfc., Army; Dillsburg, Pa.; Third Infantry Division.

2005-05-15.title: Court on Crimes in Former Yugoslavia Hits Its Stride
2005-05-15.abstract: It happens discreetly, unnoticed by other passengers. The traveler, usually a middle-aged man, gets off the plane at a Dutch airport where plainclothes officials politely receive him. He is escorted to a vehicle and, before long, he disappears behind the walls of a high-security jail near The Hague.    Thus, another war crimes suspect is checked in at the compound where the United Nations has its own cellblock.

2005-05-15.title: As Nations Lobby to Join Security Council, the U.S. Resists Giving Them Veto Power
2005-05-15.abstract: The United States has warned four nations campaigning jointly for permanent seats on the United Nations Security Council that it will not support their cause unless they agree not to ask for the veto power that the five current permanent Council members hold, senior diplomats and administration officials said.     The four nations -- Brazil, India, Germany and Japan -- are unhappy about that position. ''The Security Council is not like an aircraft, with first class, business and economy seats,'' said Ryozo Kato, Japan's ambassador to the United States.

2005-05-15.title: Karzai Says Afghan Progress Is Protests' Real Target
2005-05-15.abstract: President Hamid Karzai returned from a trip to drum up more aid from Europe on Saturday to express shame and frustration at the violence that has racked Afghanistan over the last five days, leaving at least 17 dead and many wounded.    At his heavily guarded office in Kabul, he blamed ''enemies of peace'' and ''enemies of stability'' for violently subverting students' protests over recent reports that American interrogators at Guantánamo Bay, Cuba, had desecrated a Koran.

2005-05-16.title: Adventurer finds challenge in a city of extremes
Seattle leads U.S. cities joining Kyoto Protocol
U.S. warns N. Korea on atomic test
Russian Court Suspends Reading of Verdict Against Billionaire
2005-05-16.abstract: The judges began reading their verdict against Mikhail B. Khodorkovsky, but then postponed the final verdict for at least another day.

2005-05-16.title: U.S. Is Warning North Koreans on Nuclear Test
2005-05-16.abstract: Bush administration warns North Korea that if it conducts nuclear test, United States and several Pacific powers would take punitive action, but does not specify what kind of sanctions would result; Pres Bush's national security adviser Stephen J Hadley calls North Korea 'hard target' whose intentions are nearly impossible to assess, but his warnings represent first time anyone in Bush administration has drawn 'red line' that North Korea could not cross without prompting reaction; Bush's aides have said over past year that drawing clear line would be seen by North Koreans as challenge; they say concerns about baiting North Korea could explain why Hadley did not specify what kind of penalty is possible; Shinzo Abe, secretary general of Japan's governing party, says that if North Korea conducts nuclear tests, Japan would go to Security Council before imposing sanctions; photo (M)

2005-05-17.title: Two Koreas resume talks after 10-month hiatus
Uzbeks Say Troops Shot Recklessly at Civilians
2005-05-17.abstract: Government of Uzbekistan maintains that it acted cautiously and minimized use of force in putting down prison break and demonstration, but survivors with gunshot wounds dispute this; they say security forces fired indiscriminately at unarmed civilians, striking women and children in public square in Andijon with little warning; say many civilians had gathered in square in hope of seeing Pres Islam A Karimov, to complain about joblessness and poverty; say they were met by troops instead; Karimov blames unrest on Islamic extremist groups; photo (M) with

2005-05-17.title: French Lose a Holiday, but Many, Angry, Take One Anyway
2005-05-17.abstract: France is thrown into confusion by well-meaning government initiative to sacrifice paid holiday to raise money for country's elderly; main unions urge their workers to strike, and almost half the country stays home; many town halls throughout country are closed, and dozens of cities and towns are without most public transportation; schools operate according to whimsy of their principals and teachers; government challenges assumption that initiative has failed; photo (M)

2005-05-17.title: Italian CARE worker kidnapped in Kabul
Gunmen in Afghan Capital Kidnap Italian CARE Worker
2005-05-17.abstract: Afghan security officials report kidnapping of Clementina Cantoni, Italian woman working for CARE International; say she was abducted in Kabul, second kidnapping of foreigner in six months in country; it comes after several days of worst anti-Western violence there since American intervention in wake of 9/11; just last week CARE issued report about deteriorating security in Afghanistan, which it said was hampering work of aid organizations there; photos (M)

2005-05-18.title: British Lawmaker Scolds Senators on Iraq
2005-05-18.abstract: George Galloway, maverick member of British Parliament, denies to US Senate Permanent Committee on Investigations as 'absolutely preposterous' committee's charges that Saddam Hussein's government had given him rights to buy 20 million barrels of oil to sell at profit; his appearance seems to catch Senate committee off guard; Galloway also uses committee's invitation to testify under oath to turn tables on his accusers, charging that commitee had 'found me guilty' without having given him opportunity to defend himself against allegations that he had profited from United Nations oil-for-food program; photo (M)

2005-05-18.title: Spain gives EU treaty final backing
A Silent Man Who Excels at the Piano Baffles Britain
2005-05-18.abstract: British authorities are mystified as to identity of man who was found wandering on beach in damp clothes on Isle of Sheppey in Kent; he was wearing dark suit and white shirt; labels had been cut out from his clothes so they offered no clues to his identity; he may be man suffering from amnesia after terrible shock of some kind; nothing can be confirmed as he has not spoken or communicated in words--spoken or written--since he was found; all that is known is that when people who cared for him gave him pencil and paper in early April, he drew grand piano casting deep shadow; and when they took him to piano in hospital chapel he was transformed, playing fluently in what seemed classical style; photo (M)

2005-05-18.title: Foreign Gas Companies in Bolivia Face Sharply Higher Taxes
2005-05-18.abstract: Bolivian Pres Carlos Mesa's government allows passage of bill that will sharply raise taxes on foreign energy companies, move officials hope will defuse mounting protests from groups that want Bolivia to squeeze big multinationals that have flocked to this poor country; but decision by Mesa, who has bowed repeatedly to demands from protesters in his 19-month term, appears to placate few; foreign oil companies say law is financially onerous and will prompt them to cut back on investments; thousands of Indians and miners continue to march from several communities in highlands toward capital, some of their leaders promising to take over Congress unless tougher law is approved; photo (M)

2005-05-18.title: North Koreans resist appeal to rejoin talks

2005-05-19.title: Israel Carries Out First Airstrike, in Gaza, in 3 Months of Truce
2005-05-19.abstract: Israel carries out its first airstrike since truce was announced three months ago, firing on Palestinian cell that was firing mortars at Jewish settlements in southern Gaza Strip; one Palestinian militant is dead and several are wounded, as is one Israeli civilian; events reflect fragile nature of truce that has greatly reduced overall level of violence since Feb but has not silenced the guns (M)

2005-05-19.title: Leave rules to us, sports chiefs say
Sunni Accuses a Shiite Militia of Killing Clerics
2005-05-19.abstract: Leading Sunni cleric in Iraq Sheik Harith al-Dari publicly accuses Shiite militia of killing Sunni clerics; charge seen as troubling sign of rising tensions between Iraq's two main religious groups; Dari charges that militia group known as Badr Brigade killed Sunni clerics, including one whose body was found May 15 in Shiite area of Baghdad; commander of Badr Brigade denies charge and blames hard-line Sunni Islamists and former members of Saddam Hussein's government for killings (M)

2005-05-19.title: Uneasily, a Latin Land Looks at Its Own Complexion
2005-05-19.abstract: Rev Jesse L Jackson, in visit to Mexico, says Pres Vicente Fox's recent derogatory remark about African-Americans was 'offensive' but may force Mexico to give blacks place in its negotiations with United States on variety of issues; some commentators in Mexico say Fox inadvertently exposed disturbing reality, that his comments were not uncommon among Mexicans who typically pride themselves on being colorblind society; photo (M)

2005-05-19.title: Koreas End Talks, Resuming High-Level Ties
2005-05-19.abstract: North Korea did not promise to return to six-nation talks aimed at ending the Communist state's nuclear weapons program.

2005-05-20.title: In U.S. Report, Brutal Details of 2 Afghan Inmates' Deaths
2005-05-20.abstract: A file of an inquiry into the deaths of two detainees at Bagram, Afghanistan, depicts repeated harsh treatment of prisoners.

2005-05-20.title: Japan cool to China bid for a free trade accord
Election over, Zimbabwe is back to bad old days
Israeli defense chief issues warning on rockets
Iraqi Government, in Statement With Iran, Admits Fault for 1980's War
2005-05-20.abstract: Shiite-led Iraqi government publicly acknowledges for first time that Iraq was aggressor in 1980 when it touched off bloody eight-year war with Iran; statement comes at end of three-day visit by Iranian Foreign Min Kamal Kharazi; it is likely to inflame further resentment of Iraq's Sunni Arabs, who ran country at time of that war; Iran has long sought formal recognition of Iraq's use of chemical weapons against it during war (M)

2005-05-21.title: Army file details deaths of Afghan detainees
World Briefing | Europe: Britain: Girl, 16, Held In 'Happy Slap' Attack
2005-05-21.abstract: A 16-year-old girl was arrested and charged with being part of a group of youths that attacked another girl in Manchester while filming the incident on a mobile telephone. The victim, 16-year-old Becky Smith, was briefly knocked out and spent two days in the hospital with head injuries. The assault was the latest example of an apparent British craze known as ''happy slapping,'' in which youths randomly attack passers-by and then use their mobile phones to circulate pictures of the victims. Miss Smith's mother, Georgina, said that video phones should be banned from schools.   Sarah Lyall (NYT)

2005-05-21.title: Some settlers in Gaza still plan to stay put
Prison Images Raise Issues for World Media
2005-05-21.abstract: A front-page photograph of Saddam Hussein in his underwear in an Iraqi prison greeted readers of The New York Post and The Sun in London yesterday, coming to The Sun from an American military source seeking to weaken the Iraqi insurgency, The Post reported.     The photo of Mr. Hussein in briefs and the story of how the tabloid newspapers obtained the images quickly became television news, and print journalists as well struggled with how they would handle the provocative report.

2005-05-22.title: Vatican Says Mexican Priest Will Not Face Abuse Trial
2005-05-22.abstract: The founder of a Roman Catholic order in Mexico will not face a church trial on allegations that he molested teenagers, a Vatican spokesman said.

2005-05-22.title: Army Faltered in Investigating Detainee Abuse
2005-05-22.abstract: Despite autopsy findings of homicide and statements by soldiers that two prisoners died after being struck by guards at an American military detention center in Bagram, Afghanistan, Army investigators initially recommended closing the case without bringing any criminal charges, documents and interviews show.    Within days after the two deaths in December 2002, military coroners determined that both had been caused by ''blunt force trauma'' to the legs. Soon after, soldiers and others at Bagram told the investigators that military guards had repeatedly struck both men in the thighs while they were shackled and that one had also been mistreated by military interrogators.

2005-05-22.title: Two Belgians Win Top Prize at Cannes for Second Time
2005-05-22.abstract: ''The Child,'' a Belgian film directed by Jean-Pierre Dardenne and Luc Dardenne, won the Palme d'Or as best film at the 58th Cannes Film Festival on Saturday night.    The film, which follows a young petty thief as he struggles with the moral dilemmas of fatherhood, was inspired by Dostoyevsky's ''Crime and Punishment'' and influenced by the classic French film ''Pickpocket,'' by Robert Bresson.

2005-05-22.title: North Korean ship makes historic trip to South
Charter for the European Union Meets Resistance
2005-05-22.abstract: For a quarter of a century, it has been pretty much assumed in the half-dozen founding countries of the now 25-member European Union that popular support for an ever bigger and more unified Europe would be virtually automatic.     Not anymore. At least not if large segments of public opinion in the founding states of France and the Netherlands matter.

2005-05-23.title: A new breed of evangelicals joins U.S. elite
U.S. wants to know how Saddam photos got out
Briefly: 20 hurt as bombs target controversial movie
Andes Deaths Are Another Black Eye for Chile's Military
2005-05-23.abstract: Gen Juan Emilio Cheyre, commander of Chilean Army, is being blamed by many for loss of at least 21 young, inexperienced recruits in Andean blizzard; since becoming commander three years ago, he has been one of Pres Ricardo Lagos's chief allies in working to overcome legacy of Pinochet years, apologizing for human rights abuses and repeatedly bowing to principle of civilian supremacy over armed forces at other critical moments; now grieving and irate parents wonder why their teenage sons were sent off ill prepared and ill equipped on their fatal mission; two colonels who led regiment involved in exercise have been stripped of their posts pending outcome of investigation; photo; map (M)

2005-05-24.title: Iraq Can't Explain $69 Million in Fuel Oil From '04, Audit Says
2005-05-24.abstract: Iraqi officials cannot explain what happened to $69 million worth of fuel oil produced in the second half of 2004, raising fears that it was smuggled out of the country for private gain, according to a report released yesterday by United Nations-appointed auditors.     The report, by the auditing firm KPMG, said Iraq's recorded exports of fuel oil mysteriously declined by a comparable amount during that same period of 2004, the initial months of sovereignty for the newly installed Iraqi government.

2005-05-24.title: Dragnet Arrests in Zimbabwe Send Warning
2005-05-24.abstract: With grocery stores bereft of basic goods and the currency once more in free fall, Zimbabwe's government said Monday that it had rounded up more than 9,600 residents of the capital, Harare, in a crackdown on black-market peddlers and ''general lawlessness.''    The official explanation for the dragnet, announced in state-run newspapers, was that the raids were aimed at black-market profiteers who were hoarding commodities like cooking oil and corn meal, creating artificial shortages.

2005-05-24.title: In Southern France, Strong Opposition to Europe Treaty
2005-05-24.abstract: Over sausage sandwiches and vast amounts of beer and local wine, thousands of Frenchmen stood around and argued over how best to save France. Save France from Europe, that is: the Europe that France played a crucial role in building a half-century ago.    But now, say the anti-globalizers and anti-imperialists, the farmers and factory workers who crammed into the smoky exhibition hall in this southern city Friday, Europe has lost its way. They may be treated as traitors and imbeciles by their opponents, they add, but they call themselves patriots.

2005-05-24.title: Car Bombings in Iraq Kill 33, With Shiites as Targets
2005-05-24.abstract: Insurgents carried out three major car bomb attacks against Iraqi Shiites on Monday, killing at least 33 and wounding 120 in what appeared to be the latest in a wave of violence intended to exploit the sectarian divisions that have tormented the country.    All told, attacks across Iraq killed at least 43 people, including Waiel al-Rubaie, a senior aide in Prime Minister Ibrahim al-Jaafari's administration, and his driver, who were shot to death in the Mansour district of Baghdad. The American military said three American soldiers were also killed in the northern city of Mosul on Sunday.

2005-05-25.title: China Backs Uzbek, Splitting With U.S. on Crackdown
2005-05-25.abstract: China offers unequivocal support for Uzbek Pres Islam Karimov, who faces US and other international criticism for crackdown against prison break and antigovernment rally in Andijon; support comes amid new signs that violence exceeded official accounts and warnings from rights groups of new roundups; China firmly backs crackdown on 'separatism, terrorism and extremism' in statement preceding Karimov visit; photographer working for The New York Times photographs some forty fresh, hastily dug graves outside Andijon; photos; map; doctor reportedly says she counted 400 bodies at morgue (M)

2005-05-25.title: Iran to maintain suspension of nuclear program
Iran and Europeans Jockey for Meeting on Nuclear Issue on Wednesday
2005-05-25.abstract: Iranian and European negotiators hold preliminary talks to try to salvage nuclear agreement following Iran's threats to resume activities banned under 2004 agreement (S)

2005-05-25.title: President of World Bank calls for change

2005-05-26.title: Entr'acte: For affirmative action, a daring test in the arts
Names of the Dead
2005-05-26.abstract: The Department of Defense has identified 1,636 American service members who have died since the start of the Iraq war. It confirmed the deaths of the following Americans yesterday:    CREAMEAN, Tyler L., 21, Specialist, Army; Jacksonville, Ark.; 25th Infantry Division.    HAHN, Peter J., 31, Sgt. First Class, Army National Guard; Metairie, La.; Second Battalion, 156th Infantry, 256th Brigade Combat Team.    MORGAIN, Carl J., 40, Sgt., Army National Guard; Butler, Pa.; First Battalion, 112th Infantry.    OGBURN, John B. III, 45, Sgt., Army National Guard; Fruitland, Ore.; Third Battalion, 116th Armored Cavalry.    PEREZ, Christopher S., 30, Sgt., Marines; Hutchinson, Kan.; Second Marine Division.    SEESAN, Aaron N., 25, First Lt., Army; of Ohio; 25th Infantry Division.    WENTZ, Brad A., 21, Sgt., Army Reserve; Gladwin, Mich.; 180th Transportation Company.    WILKERSON, Charles T., 30, Sgt., Army; Kansas City, Mo.; Third Armored Cavalry.

2005-05-26.title: After French vote: A pivotal point if 'no' wins
Bush Offers Abbas Public Support and More Aid
2005-05-26.abstract: President Bush praised Mahmoud Abbas and pledged millions of dollars in direct American aid for the Palestinian Authority.

2005-05-27.title: Mongolians test alien idea: Privatizing the land
World Briefing | Middle East: Egypt: Multicandidate Vote Approved
2005-05-27.abstract: Voters overwhelmingly approved a constitutional change to allow Egypt's first contested presidential election, Interior Minister Habib el-Adly announced on state-run television. He said that 83 percent of the voters in the referendum on Wednesday had backed the change and that 54 percent of the country's 32 million registered voters had participated. Opposition figures -- who had called for a boycott of the referendum -- quickly dismissed the figures. In Washington, President Bush criticized the government of President Hosni Mubarak for the violence during the voting, including the beating of opposition demonstrators. ''The idea of people expressing themselves in opposition to the government, then getting beaten, is not our view of how a democracy ought to work,'' Mr. Bush said. ''It's not the way that you have free elections.''   Hassan M. Fattah (NYT)

2005-05-27.title: Comments by a Tokyo diplomat miff Seoul

2005-05-28.title: EU urges Dutch and French to say 'yes'
Turkish lawmakers push to revise criminal code
Berlusconi supports Blair's goals for G8
Internet Site Says Zarqawi Is Healthy and in Charge
2005-05-28.abstract: A posting on Friday on the Internet site for Al Qaeda in Mesopotamia, the group led by the Islamic militant Abu Musab al-Zarqawi, reversed two days of dire warnings about his condition and stated that he was ''in good health and runs the jihad operation by himself,'' according to the SITE Institute, a group that tracks the postings.    It was one in a series of conflicting statements in recent days about Mr. Zarqawi's health. On Tuesday, his group said he had been seriously injured and urged Muslims to pray for his recovery. On Thursday, postings on two different Islamist sites said after suffering a bullet wound in his lung, he had appointed a deputy to stand in for him and left Iraq to seek medical care.

2005-05-29.title: Sated With Snuff, Swedes Take Smoking Ban in Stride
2005-05-29.abstract: Sweden is days away from banning smoking in restaurants and bars, but the national passion for snuff will likely mean that the measure will be met with little protest.

2005-05-29.title: Across Iran, Nuclear Power Is a Matter of Pride
2005-05-29.abstract: From nuclear negotiators to student dissidents, from bazaar merchants to turbaned mullahs, Iranians agree: the right to develop nuclear power is a point of national pride.     ''For a country to have nuclear energy means that it has made progress in all other fields as well, so other countries have to respect its technology,'' said Nilufar, 29, a graduate student in energy management at the prestigious Sharif Industrial University. Nilufar, covered in black so only her face was showing, agreed to be interviewed on such a delicate topic only if her family name was not used.

2005-05-29.title: Forensic officers search for clues amid debris of Indonesian bombs
Violence Surges Across Iraq With 30 New Deaths Reported
2005-05-29.abstract: The surge of violence that has swept Iraq since its first elected government took office nearly a month ago continued Saturday, with at least 30 new deaths reported across the country, some of them in what appeared to be sectarian killings.    The latest attacks raised the total number of Iraqis killed this month to about 650, in addition to at least 63 American troops who have been killed, the highest American toll since January.

2005-05-29.title: Scenes From a Nightmare: A Shrine to the Maoist Chaos
2005-05-29.abstract: Nothing but the faint sound of birds nesting on surrounding hilltops can be heard inside this new mountaintop site -- part museum, part monument -- that is the first public commemoration of one of the darkest chapters in China's recent past.    Inside the circular pavilion that is the site's centerpiece, the walls are lined with a series of gray tablets, each starkly engraved with images depicting the Cultural Revolution, China's decade-long descent into madness, beginning in the mid-1960's.

2005-05-30.title: Turnout Is Low as Lebanon Begins Voting for Parliament
2005-05-30.abstract: Voters here kicked off the first of four rounds of voting for Parliament on Sunday in the first elections free of Syrian interference in more than three decades. A powerful slate led by the son of the assassinated former prime minister is heavily favored to sweep the vote.     Mohammad Taqoush, 25, went to the polls in the Ras Beirut neighborhood of West Beirut with high expectations. He marched in the demonstrations against the Syrian presence in Lebanon after the assassination of the former prime minister, Rafik Hariri, in February, and saw the vote as a moment for the former opposition to make good on its promises for change.

2005-05-30.title: Senior cleric is murdered in Kandahar
Gunmen Kill Afghan Cleric Who Condemned Taliban Leaders
2005-05-30.abstract: A senior pro-government cleric was shot dead in his office on Sunday in the southern city of Kandahar in a brazen attack by suspected Taliban supporters firing from a motorbike.    In Kabul, meanwhile, kidnappers of an Italian aid worker, Clementina Cantoni, released a videotape of her to a television station and demanded that the Italian authorities speed up negotiations for her release.

2005-05-30.title: Sulawesi churches guarded after market bombs kill 20

2005-05-31.title: What next for Tony Blair: A referendum for Britain or flight?
In Political Step, Pope Confronts Law on Fertility
2005-05-31.abstract: Pope Benedict XVI endorses call by Italian bishops for boycott of referendum in Italy on medically assisted fertility; referendum seeks to overturn key provisions in most restrictive law on medically assisted fertility in Europe; law, passed last year, bans donations of sperm and eggs, defines life as beginning at conception, and allows fertility treatment only to married heterosexual couples; effort to roll back many provisions is shaping up as important battleground for Roman Catholic Church, energized by new pope with strong views on social issues; many in Italy, which is overwhelmingly Catholic, support law and church's engagement to protect it; others worry that church is making disturbing intrusion into politics; photos (M)

2005-05-31.title: China Says Hong Kong Journalist It Holds Is a Spy
2005-05-31.abstract: China on Tuesday accused of prominent reporter for a Singaporean newspaper of spying for foreign intelligence agencies, making its first official comment on the matter more than a month after the reporter was detained by security officials.    The reporter, Ching Cheong, China correspondent of Singapore's s main newspaper, The Straits Times, and a resident of Hong Kong, was seized by the authorities on April 22 while visiting the southern province of Guangzhou, said his wife, Mary Lau.
